## Data Preparation

### Input Format

The pipeline consumes time-indexed LOB snapshot data. Each row represents
an order-book event with multi-level bid/ask prices, sizes, and order counts,
plus trade descriptors used for order-flow features. The core column groups
are summarized below.

Table: Input schema used in the empirical pipeline

| Column group | Description | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `timestamp` | Chronological event index | Ordering, temporal features |
| `bid_px_NN`, `ask_px_NN` | Bid/ask prices at level `NN` | Spread, mid-price, slope, valuation |
| `bid_sz_NN`, `ask_sz_NN` | Resting size at level `NN` | Imbalance, depth, OFI, queue features |
| `bid_ct_NN`, `ask_ct_NN` | Order count at level `NN` | Order count imbalance |
| `action`, `side`, `size` | Event type, aggressor side, size | Signed trade flow features |
| `close` (auxiliary) | Mid-price-like pricing series | Reward and portfolio valuation |

### Preprocessing and Splitting

Raw data is validated for chronological order, required column presence,
non-negative prices, and non-crossed best quotes before any feature
engineering. This stage is conservative: its purpose is correctness, not
statistical filtering.

The dataset is then split chronologically into training, validation, and test
subsets. Shuffling is not used because it would destroy temporal structure
and introduce information from future periods into earlier training samples.
The training split is used to fit model parameters; the validation split
supports configuration comparison and development diagnostics; the test split
is reserved for final out-of-sample reporting and does not influence any
model selection decisions.

### Feature Normalization and Leakage Prevention

Normalization statistics (mean and standard deviation for z-score scaling)
are estimated on the training split only and applied unchanged to the
validation and test splits. Fitting normalization on the full dataset would
allow future volatility regimes to influence the training representation,
inflating apparent performance. Cyclical time encodings (hour-of-day sine and
cosine) are already bounded in $[-1, 1]$ and are not normalized. Raw price
columns used for portfolio accounting are kept in their original units and are
never replaced by normalized values.

### Feature and Price Column Separation

A strict separation is maintained between the observation features passed to
the learning algorithm and the price inputs used by the portfolio simulation.
The learning features are normalized and engineered; the pricing columns are
economically interpretable and used only for reward and valuation computation.
Mixing these two roles can produce invalid reward calculations or cause
normalized prices to appear in financial metrics.

In the current implementation, the auxiliary pricing series used for portfolio
valuation plays the role of a mid-price proxy rather than an executable bid/ask
quote. This simplifies reward accounting but makes the resulting backtest
performance optimistic relative to realistic spread-crossing execution.
